perturbation.original_text: Vendor agrees not to communicate, disclose, or otherwise make available all or any part of the Proprietary Information to any third party, including, but not limited to Vendor's parent, subsidiaries, or affiliated companies.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor agrees not to communicate, disclose, or otherwise make available all or any part of the Proprietary Information to any third party, including Vendor's parent, subsidiaries, or affiliated companies, unless such disclosure is required by law.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless such disclosure is required by law' could be interpreted to allow disclosure even if it violates trade secret laws, especially if the definition of 'required by law' is not clearly defined. This creates ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. While 'required by law' seems innocuous, if interpreted broadly, it could allow disclosure in situations not genuinely mandated by law, thus undermining trade secret protection. The added clause introduces uncertainty about what constitutes a legally required disclosure, potentially conflicting with the intended protection of trade secrets under CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The added clause 'unless such disclosure is required by law' in the changed text introduces ambiguity and potential conflict with trade secret protection as described in California Civil Code 3426.1. While seemingly innocuous, the Law Explanation suggests that a broad interpretation of 'required by law' could permit disclosures not genuinely mandated, undermining the protection of trade secrets. The original text had no such exception and so provided greater protection. Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' to include 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By adding the exception, the changed text potentially allows for disclosure without consent if someone interprets it as legally required, thus creating a significant contradiction of the original contract and the intended protections under CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor agrees to make no more than five (5) copies of the Proprietary Information unless otherwise agreed in writing between the parties; and Vendor agrees to limit distribution of and access to the Proprietary Information to those of Vendor's personnel who require access to Proprietary Information for the foregoing purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor agrees to make as many copies of the Proprietary Information as necessary for its business operations; and Vendor agrees to limit distribution of and access to the Proprietary Information to those of Vendor's personnel who deem it necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the copy restriction and access control allows for more widespread distribution, potentially violating trade secret laws by increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Removing the copy limit and allowing access based on personnel's subjective 'deeming' undermines reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, increasing the risk of misappropriation and violating CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts CUTSA by undermining reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The original text limited copies to five and restricted access, while the changed text allows unlimited copies and access based on subjective necessity. This contradicts the principle of maintaining secrecy as highlighted in California Civil Code 3426.1, which defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure and potential misappropriation. The changed text fails to provide adequate safeguards, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor and Ingram mutually agree that all copies of the Proprietary Information and all written descriptions, extractions, or summaries thereof, whether made by Vendor or Ingram, shall be the property of Ingram, and shall, upon expiration of this Agreement or Ingram's request, be immediately returned to Ingram.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor and Ingram mutually agree that all copies of the Proprietary Information and all written descriptions, extractions, or summaries thereof, whether made by Vendor or Ingram, shall be the property of Ingram, unless Vendor believes such information is essential for its continued business operations, in which case Vendor shall retain a copy.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing the vendor to retain a copy of proprietary information after the agreement ends contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and could enable misappropriation of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA provides remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets, which can occur when a party continues to use trade secrets after authorization has ended. Allowing the vendor to retain a copy based on their subjective belief weakens the protection against misappropriation and conflicts with the intent of CUTSA to prevent unauthorized use of trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Vendor to retain proprietary information based on a subjective belief, which contradicts the intent of trade secret protection laws. The original agreement mandates the return of proprietary information upon expiration or request, but the modified version weakens this obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CIV 3426.2(a)) allows for injunctions against misappropriation. By allowing the vendor to retain a copy, the changed contract increases the risk of misappropriation, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that any provision is found invalid or unenforceable pursuant to Domestic Distribution Agreement March 15, 1996 Form 403 Ingram Micro (3/15/96) 108 <PAGE> 13 statutory or Judicial decree, such provision shall be construed only to the maximum extent permitted by law, and the remainder of the Agreement shall be valid and enforceable in accordance with its terms.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that any provision is found invalid or unenforceable pursuant to statutory or Judicial decree, such provision shall be automatically replaced with a new provision, at Vendor's discretion, that achieves a similar outcome, and the remainder of the Agreement shall be valid and enforceable in accordance with its terms.
perturbation.explanation: Giving the vendor unilateral authority to replace unenforceable provisions introduces ambiguity and could lead to the vendor substituting provisions that are detrimental to Ingram Micro and potentially violate California contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1654 states that in cases of uncertainty, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist. Giving the Vendor the power to unilaterally replace a clause creates uncertainty in the application of the contract, potentially hurting Ingram. This change contradicts this principle of contractual interpretation under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1654 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1654. In cases of uncertainty not removed by the preceding rules, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist. (Amended by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Vendor to unilaterally replace unenforceable provisions with a new provision 'at Vendor's discretion, that achieves a similar outcome'. This introduces uncertainty that could be detrimental to Ingram Micro. California Civil Code § 1654, as quoted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that 'In cases of uncertainty not removed by the preceding rules, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist.' By granting the Vendor unilateral power, the changed text creates uncertainty in the contract's application, which contradicts the principle of interpreting against the party causing the uncertainty. The Vendor causing the uncertainty could lead to interpretations favorable to the Vendor but detrimental to Ingram Micro, thus contradicting the intent of the law.
